sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 3 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP, UP, SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 31, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: SPACE expanded the white block (rows 36-40) but didn't collect the left target (rows 28-31). Player is still below the target. Need to move UP more to get adjacent.
- Goal model: Navigate the player to interact with the targets. Likely need to reach the center target first.
- Plan: Move UP 2 more times to reach the left target, then SPACE.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 30 of about 71 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
After UP, UP, SPACE:
- id=7: color='G' (dark gray), rows 32-35, cols 16-19, pixels=12 - this is a dark gray frame
- id=8: color='b' (blue), rows 33-34, cols 17-18, pixels=4 - blue center inside id=7
- id=9: color='W' (white), row 36, cols 16-19, pixels=4 - white block
- id=10: color='N' (green), rows 37-39, cols 16-19 - green base
- Left target (id=3) still at rows 28-31, cols 16-19
The player moved UP (from rows 36-43 to rows 32-39), and SPACE caused the white block to "reset" again (became a dark gray frame with blue center, similar to the target structure). The left target is still not collected.
I notice a pattern: SPACE seems to create a "copy" of the target structure below the player, but doesn't actually collect the target. The only time a target was collected was the first SPACE when the center target was adjacent.
Wait, let me reconsider. The first time SPACE was pressed:
- Player was at rows 36-43 (white at 36-40, green at 41-43)
- Center target was at rows 36-39
- After SPACE, the white block expanded and the target disappeared
But now:
- Player is at rows 32-39
- Left target is at rows 28-31
- After SPACE, a dark gray frame appeared at rows 32-35 (where the white block was)
- Left target still exists at rows 28-31
The difference is that the first time, the player's white block was AT the same position as the target (rows 36-39 vs rows 36-40). Now the player is below the target.
I need to move UP one more time to get the player adjacent to the left target. The target is at rows 28-31, and the player's dark gray frame is at rows 32-35. One UP would move the player to rows 28-35, which would overlap with the target.
But wait, the player structure changed. Now id=7 is the dark gray frame (G) at rows 32-35, and id=9 is the white block at row 36. The white block is no longer on top of the green base - it's between the dark gray frame and the green base.
Let me try UP one more time to get closer to the target, then SPACE.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Player moved to rows 32-39. SPACE created a dark gray frame (id=7) at rows 32-35 but didn't collect the left target at rows 28-31. The white block (id=9) is now at row 36, between the frame and green base. Need to get closer to the target.
Plan: Move UP one more time to align with the left target, then SPACE.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player frame at rows 32-35, target at rows 28-31
# One UP should move player to rows 28-31, overlapping with target
result = action(['UP', 'SPACE'])
print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")
print(f"Done: {last_action_result.get('done', False)}")
print(f"Reward: {last_action_result.get('reward', 0)}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    b = n['boundary']
    rows = [p[0] for p in b]
    cols = [p[1] for p in b]
    print(f"  id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}], pixels={n['pixels']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
